title: Agent unable to reach fixerator.com — Chrome extension permission gate blocks all navigation
description: All navigation attempts to https://fixerator.com and https://www.fixerator.com timed out with the message 'The navigate tool did not respond in time. The Chrome extension is connected but the page may be loading, unresponsive, or waiting on a permission prompt in the extension side panel.' Tabs remained on chrome://newtab/ across 6+ retries with waits of 3-10s. tabs_context_mcp confirms ALL 10 visible tabs (mine and other parallel agents') are stuck on chrome://newtab — implying every parallel agent in this sweep hit the same gate. curl -sI https://fixerator.com returns HTTP/2 307 → 200 OK, so the origin is healthy; the block is in the Chrome MCP extension's domain-permission step.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech who only has 15 minutes between service calls, I could not load the site at all. If a real expert had this experience they would close the tab and never return — every parallel agent sharing this Chrome appears blocked, so this is not a single-session quirk.
expected: Navigation to fixerator.com to succeed for whitelisted agent tabs, the same way it does for the existing tab on localhost:3000/design-system.
fix_recommendation: Before launching parallel user-testing sweeps, pre-grant the MCP extension's domain permission for fixerator.com (and the www subdomain) on the test Chrome profile — or have a human accept the permission prompt once at sweep start. Document the prerequisite in user-testing/2026-05-11-design-redesign/README.md.

title: Hero copy 'I'm here to terminate your project' lands as menacing to anxious first-timer
description: Landing hero reads in big bold dark-mode: 'Hi, I'm Fix. I'm here to terminate your project.' with 'terminate' in red/orange. Subtitle clarifies 'I'll be back… with the receipts from Home Depot' but that's a Terminator reference, and the tooltip-style line is much smaller and grey. As a first-timer who came here because something is broken and I'm afraid I'll make it worse, the word 'terminate' in red read like 'this app will destroy my project'. I had to read it twice and look at the robot to figure out the joke. Once you 'get it' it's clever; before you get it, the dominant signal is hostility.
user_impact: I almost bounced. I'm here because I'm worried, and the very first thing the page says is that something will be terminated. The joke landed only after I'd read more — at the cost of my initial confidence in the product.
expected: A first-time visitor in beginner mode should see reassurance ('I'll walk you through it', 'No DIY experience needed') before brand-voice jokes.
fix_recommendation: Either A/B test a softer first-visit headline (e.g. 'I'm here to help you finish your project'), or pair 'terminate' with a clearer in-line wink ('finish — yes, I mean finish'). The current copy assumes the user already understands the Terminator brand voice — anxious beginners do not.

title: Free-form 'my sink is leaking' answer skips the water-shutoff safety step
description: I typed 'my sink is leaking underneath and I don't know what to do' into Ask Anything. The AI gave a great empathetic opener ('I'm sorry to hear that! ... don't worry — this is a very common DIY repair') and then listed 5 causes (P-trap, drain basket, supply line, faucet body, garbage disposal) with prices and a diagnosis flow (dry, run water, watch). But it did NOT tell me anywhere in the response to 'turn off the shut-off valves under the sink before you touch anything'. The Project-Template path for 'Replace a Faucet' put 'Step 1: Prep & Shut Off Water' first, which is exactly right. The free-form chat path lacks that safety floor for beginners.
user_impact: As an anxious first-timer my #1 fear is making the leak worse and flooding my kitchen. The single thing I needed before anything else was 'turn off the water'. The AI gave me diagnosis and price ranges but never told me to stop the water flow first. If I'd tried to follow the Fix section under 'P-Trap Leak' ('Tighten the slip-joint nuts by hand') I might have done it with the water on.
expected: For any beginner-flavored plumbing question, the response should lead with a one-line 'Before you touch anything, turn off the shut-off valves under your sink (turn clockwise)'.
fix_recommendation: Inject a safety preamble into plumbing-leak-style responses (similar to the well-done Step 1 in the Replace-a-Faucet template flow). Detect 'leak / leaking / dripping / water' in the prompt and lead with the shutoff instruction.

title: Jargon in chat response not fully explained for beginner ('slip-joint nuts', 'shut-off valves', 'faucet cartridge')
description: In the sink-leak response, P-trap is helpfully glossed ('The curved pipe under your sink that holds water to block sewer gases'). But other terms drop unexplained: 'slip-joint nuts' (Fix bullet under P-Trap), 'shut-off valves' (mentioned without locating), 'faucet cartridge' (Fix bullet under Faucet Body Leak), 'plumber's putty or Teflon tape' (no guidance which to use).
user_impact: After the great P-trap definition I thought 'okay, this app explains things to me' — and then immediately bumped into terms I didn't know. It eroded the goodwill from the opening reassurance and made me think 'I'm not going to know what I'm looking at under there'.
expected: Consistent jargon-handling: if you're going to define one piece of terminology, define them all. Or hover-tooltips for trade terms.
fix_recommendation: In the AI system prompt for chat with beginner-style cues, add: 'When introducing any trade term, follow it with a 1-line plain-English gloss in parentheses or a short sentence.'

title: Talk to a Pro Submit button is disabled with no inline reason for beginner
description: On the landing's 'Talk to a Pro' tab I wrote a 209-char beginner question and clicked the red 'Submit Question' button at the bottom of the form. Nothing happened — no toast, no inline error, no scroll-to-error. JS inspection shows the button is disabled=true (no title, no aria-disabled message). The Payment Method block above shows 'Save Payment Method' as a separate button. There is also a copy 'Free question used — this question costs $5-$8' which I don't understand because I have never asked an expert question on this account.
user_impact: I'd given the form my real question and clicked the obvious next step. The page silently refuses. As an anxious first-timer I assumed I'd done something wrong, but the page wouldn't tell me what. I'd give up.
expected: If Submit is disabled because a payment method isn't saved, the button should either (a) be replaced by a single 'Save Payment Method to Submit' CTA, or (b) on click show an inline message 'Save a payment method first — you won't be charged until an expert claims your question.' Also the 'Free question used' string is wrong/confusing on a fresh user — clarify or remove.
fix_recommendation: Either: route the disabled-submit click to focus the Payment Method block and show a tooltip; or change the submit button label dynamically to 'Save Payment Method to Submit'. Audit the 'Free question used' copy for accounts with 0 prior questions.

title: Find an Expert page shows experts with 0 reviews, identical $75/hr, placeholder names (incl. 'Your Mom, CA')
description: Browsing /experts as a beginner shows 8 cards: Slizzy Industry (Portsmouth NH), Mike Thompson (Austin TX), Sarah the Electrician (Denver CO), Dave the Plumber (Chicago IL), Lisa the HVAC Tech (Minneapolis MN), Tony the General Contractor (Austin TX), Willy's Welding (Your Mom, CA), Mike the Carpenter (Portland OR). Every single one shows 0 stars, '(0)' reviews, and $75/hr. One location is literally 'Your Mom, CA'. The /about page shows much more believable expert profiles ('Mike T. … 4.9 (47 reviews) … $2,400/mo avg') so the data-model exists — but the actual marketplace shows placeholder/test data.
user_impact: Trust gone. As a nervous first-timer I'd been told 'verified experts' on the homepage and About page, then the actual experts page looks like a demo account stuffed with seed data. The 'Your Mom, CA' location made me laugh out loud and then immediately distrust everything else. If I'm going to put my credit card down to ask a question, I need to believe these are real human plumbers.
expected: Either curate the seed experts (real-looking names/cities/specialties only, scrub anything that says 'Your Mom' or 'Slizzy Industry') OR badge the page as 'Demo data — production experts coming soon'. Reconcile the optimistic About page with the realistic marketplace.
fix_recommendation: Clean the production experts table: replace 'Your Mom, CA' with a real city, remove obvious placeholder names like 'Slizzy Industry', and seed some ratings/review counts so the all-zero state doesn't read as fake. If this is intentional pre-launch state, gate the /experts page behind a beta notice.

title: My Questions vs Ask Anything not differentiated for a beginner
description: In the new sidebar layout, there's an 'Ask Anything' CTA on the landing AND a sidebar item 'My Questions' under EXPERTS. As a beginner who just had a long Ask Anything conversation about my sink, I clicked My Questions expecting to see that chat. Instead I got an empty state: 'You haven't asked any questions yet'. There's no explanation that 'My Questions' is for paid expert questions, separate from the AI chat history.
user_impact: I lost the conversation I'd just had. I thought My Questions = my chat. Then I had no idea where my AI chat lived (turns out Recent Conversations on the home page).
expected: Either rename to clarify ('Expert Questions' or 'Paid Q&A') or add an empty-state explainer: 'Questions you submit to a verified expert appear here. Looking for your AI chats? Go to Home → Recent Conversations.'
fix_recommendation: Tweak the empty-state copy on /marketplace/qa (the My Questions panel) and consider renaming the sidebar entry to 'Expert Questions' or 'My Pro Questions' to disambiguate from the AI 'Ask Anything' chat.

title: Mascot positioning: drill held aggressively toward the headline
description: The Fix robot on the landing holds a drill that points back toward the words 'terminate your project' — combined with the red eye and the orange 'terminate', the visual reads as a robot pointing a tool at its own headline. Cute on its own; combined with the Terminator-style copy it amplifies the hostile-first-impression I described in finding 1.
user_impact: Reinforced the 'is this app actually going to help me, or break my house?' feeling on first scan.
expected: Either reposition the drill so it points down/away, or pair the mascot with a friendlier secondary pose (waving) for first-time visitors.
fix_recommendation: In src/components for the landing mascot, consider an idle animation that lowers the drill, or swap to a non-tool pose for the initial frame.

title: Dark warm-brown theme felt premium, not threatening
description: The body background is rgb(42,37,32) — warm dark brown, not pure black — with cream text rgb(232,223,208). Strong contrast (AAA-grade body text) and warm tonality made the app feel like a tool with character, not a sterile robot. Even as an anxious beginner I read the theme as 'serious craft tool' not 'scary tech app'.
user_impact: Even though the copy made me jumpy, the visual design didn't. The warm dark palette read as approachable.

title: AI opens with empathy and reassurance — exactly what a beginner needs
description: For my 'my sink is leaking underneath and I don't know what to do' question, the AI opened: 'I'm sorry to hear that! A leaking sink is stressful, but don't worry — this is a very common DIY repair. Let me help you figure out what's going on.' That single sentence reframed the whole experience.
user_impact: From hostile-headline anxiety to 'okay this thing might actually help me'. The empathy in the AI's voice does the persona-adaptation work that the brand voice doesn't.

title: Project Templates path produces a beginner-perfect step-by-step (Replace a Faucet)
description: Clicked the 'Replace a Faucet' template card on /about. It auto-typed 'I want to replace my kitchen faucet with a new one. What tools do I need and what are the steps?' and produced an excellent 6-step response: Step 1 leads with 'Prep & Shut Off Water' (the safety floor missing from the free-form leak response), tools are in a table with PURPOSE for each, Pro Tips include 'Take a photo of your existing plumbing before disconnecting anything' and 'Most new faucets come with supply lines — check the box before buying extras'. This is exactly what an anxious beginner needs.
user_impact: Felt completely guided. If I'd entered through the template instead of the free-form chat, I would have had a much better first experience.
fix_recommendation: Consider surfacing project templates more prominently on the landing for first-time visitors — the templates produce safer, more structured responses than free-form prompts.

title: Shopping and My Questions side-panels have clean, instructive empty states
description: Shopping empty state: 'No shopping lists yet — Start a chat, ask for a materials list, then save it to a project. Go to chat'. This tells me both what is missing AND what to do next. Same panel pattern in My Questions ('You haven't asked any questions yet'), though that one needs better copy (see finding 6).
user_impact: Empty states felt like a guide, not a dead end. Reduced anxiety about 'am I doing this right?'

title: 'Free question used' copy shows on accounts with no prior expert questions
description: On the Talk to a Pro form, the price block reads 'Free question used — this question costs $5–$8. You're only charged when an expert claims your question.' As a beginner I have never submitted any expert question on this session/account. The copy implies I burned a freebie I never knew I had.
user_impact: Made me feel tricked. As an anxious first-timer the first thought was 'wait, when did I use my free question? Did clicking 'Ask Anything' on the homepage count?' If yes, that's surprising and should be telegraphed up-front. If no, the copy is wrong.
expected: For new accounts the price block should read 'Your first question is free' or omit the 'used' framing until it has actually been used.
fix_recommendation: Check the eligibility logic for the free-question allowance and the price-block copy in the QA submission form. Either grant a clear free question to new accounts (and show 'First question free') or remove the 'used' phrasing for accounts that have never submitted.

title: Unrendered LaTeX in AI chat response — $$Z_{double shear}…$$ leaks raw
description: In the second AI response on a structural-repair conversation, an inline equation appears as raw LaTeX: $$Z_{double shear} \approx 1.75 \times Z_{single} \approx \mathbf{700–750 \text{ lbs/bolt}}$$. The KaTeX/MathJax pipeline isn't catching block-math delimiters. The rest of the response renders fine, including tables and ASCII bolt-pattern diagrams.
user_impact: As an expert I can still parse the LaTeX source, but it makes the AI look amateurish — like the team forgot to install a markdown extension. For an audience that values polish in technical references, this undercuts the credibility of an otherwise excellent answer.
expected: Block math delimiters ($$...$$) should render to formatted math, or be omitted from the model prompt template if rendering isn't supported.
fix_recommendation: Add KaTeX/MathJax to the markdown renderer used in the chat response component, OR adjust the system prompt to suppress LaTeX block-math generation. Inline $...$ may also need handling.

title: Hero block doesn't collapse after first chat message — eats 380px viewport
description: After sending the first message to the AI on the landing page, the 'Hi, I'm Fix. I'm here to terminate your project.' hero with mascot stays pinned above the conversation. On a 1252x952 viewport the hero consumes roughly 380px before the conversation begins, forcing scroll. The follow-up textarea is also pushed below the fold.
user_impact: As an expert I want my answer to be the focus once a conversation is underway — the marketing copy is fine for landing but becomes friction during use. I had to scroll repeatedly to switch between reading the response and typing a follow-up.
expected: Once the first message is sent, the hero should collapse, shrink, or move into the sidebar — giving the conversation primary screen real estate.
fix_recommendation: In the landing-page chat experience, transition from 'pre-chat' (hero visible) to 'in-chat' (hero collapsed or hidden) after the first user message. Consider an animated collapse for delight.

title: "Ask a Paid Question" submit fails for guests with no upfront warning
description: On /marketplace/qa I filled out a 576-character technical question (carpentry category) and clicked 'Submit Question'. The form had explicit messaging 'Your first question is free! No payment method needed — just ask your question and submit.' On submit I got an inline error: 'Please sign in to ask a question.' The auth gate was not surfaced before I invested in typing.
user_impact: As an expert who values efficiency, this wasted ~5 minutes of detailed question composition. Worse, the 'No payment method needed' messaging implied no friction — discovering the sign-in gate at submit time felt like a bait-and-switch.
expected: Either gate the form behind sign-in (with clear copy: 'Sign in to ask your first free question') OR allow guest submission and email-verify after. The current flow optimizes for the wrong moment.
fix_recommendation: Add an explicit 'You'll need to sign in to submit' notice above the form for guests, OR show the sign-in modal *before* allowing the user to type. Bonus: preserve the typed question across the auth flow.

title: Sidebar links to /projects, /shopping, /tools all 404 (no graceful auth gate)
description: As a signed-out user, the AppSidebar still shows DIY menu items (My Projects, My Tools, Shopping). Clicking any of them lands on a bare '404 / This page could not be found.' with no sidebar nav and no explanation. The 404 page strips the AppShell entirely except the FIXERATOR mark — looks broken.
user_impact: As an expert exploring the app I clicked My Projects expecting to either sign in or see a sign-up promo. Instead I hit a stark 404. This signals broken navigation and reduces my trust in the platform.
expected: Either (a) hide DIY menu links when signed out, (b) redirect /projects, /shopping, /tools to a sign-in screen with messaging, or (c) keep the AppShell visible on 404 with a 'Sign in to see your projects' CTA.
fix_recommendation: Add server-side auth checks on these routes that redirect to /login?next=/projects (etc.) when unauthenticated. Alternatively, conditionally render the DIY menu items only when user is signed in.

title: Production expert profiles show 'Test account for…' bios
description: On /experts the production marketplace shows seed accounts including 'Mike the Carpenter' (Portland, OR) with bio: 'Test account for carpentry user testing agent.' Other names like 'Slizzy Industry' and 'Willy's Welding' in 'Your Mom, CA' are clearly test seed data. All experts show $75/hr flat rate and 0 reviews.
user_impact: As an expert evaluating whether to trust the marketplace, seeing 'Test account for…' in a bio destroys credibility. The marketplace looks like a development sandbox, not a real product.
expected: Either (a) production should have a clean set of seeded experts with realistic bios, (b) clearly label the marketplace as 'Demo mode' until real experts are onboarded, or (c) suppress test accounts based on a flag.
fix_recommendation: Add an is_test_account flag on expert profiles and filter them out of public listing pages in production. Or update seed bios to look professional. Also clean up 'Your Mom, CA' placeholders.

title: /about page renders in light theme while rest of app is dark — jarring inconsistency
description: Navigating from any dark-themed Fixerator page to /about flips to a cream/light theme with a duplicate top header ('FIXERATOR™ About Become an Expert Get Started') in addition to the AppShell sidebar. The result is two competing layouts and a sudden visual context shift.
user_impact: As an expert mid-flow, the theme flip was disorienting — it felt like I'd navigated to a different product. The duplicate header is also visually noisy.
expected: Either the entire app is dark (and /about should be ported to the new dark design system), or /about should not show the AppShell sidebar (it's a marketing page).
fix_recommendation: Decide whether /about is a marketing page (own layout, no sidebar) or an in-app page (sidebar visible, dark theme). Currently it's neither. Probably easiest: wrap /about in the new dark AppShell.

title: About page claims 'No signup required' but Q&A submission requires sign-in
description: Top of /about reads 'Free to use · Instant answers · No signup required · Verified experts'. However the paid-Q&A submission flow on /marketplace/qa requires sign-in (see diy-expert-3). The claim is technically true for AI chat only.
user_impact: As an expert I noticed the contradiction once I hit the auth wall later. The marketing copy oversells the frictionless experience.
expected: Caveat the claim: 'No signup required for AI chat' or 'Sign in to ask experts (free first question)'.
fix_recommendation: Tweak the about-page tagline to be precise about which features require sign-up. Avoid blanket 'No signup required' that turns out to be partly true.

title: /design-system is publicly accessible in production with no auth
description: Navigating to /design-system on production loads the internal Fixerator component library page (color tokens, swatches). This is a developer-facing route that doesn't need to be publicly indexable.
user_impact: As an expert this is a minor curiosity, not a blocker. But shipping internal pages publicly is a polish/professionalism flag — also potentially SEO-pollutes the site.
expected: Either auth-gate /design-system, or add a noindex meta tag, or move it to a separate route prefix like /_dev/design-system.
fix_recommendation: Add a NEXT_PUBLIC_ENABLE_DESIGN_SYSTEM env-flagged check in the page so it 404s in production. Or add a robots.txt rule + noindex meta.

title: AI initial chat response takes 20+ seconds to start streaming
description: After submitting my first 90-word technical question on the landing chat, no content appeared in the response area for ~20 seconds — only a spinner icon. The eventual response was excellent (and long), but the initial latency before streaming begins felt much longer than chat services I'm used to.
user_impact: As an expert who values efficiency, 20 seconds with no token-by-token feedback made me wonder if my message had failed. I almost re-submitted. For complex queries that the model is 'thinking through' this may be unavoidable — but users need a 'thinking…' indicator with more reassurance than a tiny spinner.
expected: Either faster first-token latency, or clearer 'Thinking…' / 'Researching codes…' visual feedback during the wait.
fix_recommendation: If using extended-thinking on the chat endpoint, surface that with an explicit 'Reasoning…' state. Or pre-stream a 'Looking up code references…' acknowledgment before the main response begins.

title: Sidebar mascot image partially clips right edge on standard widescreen viewport
description: On the landing hero, the FIX-3000 mascot image is positioned at right and partially clips the right edge of its container — the rightmost element (looks like an exhaust/handle detail) is cut off on a 1252x952 viewport.
user_impact: As an expert I noticed because the mascot is a brand asset. Looks slightly unfinished, like the asset isn't sized to its frame.
expected: Either contain the mascot fully or pad its container to the right.
fix_recommendation: Inspect the hero layout grid — likely the mascot container needs object-contain or padding-right adjustment. Check at various viewport widths.

title: Mascot voice 'terminate your project' lands as Terminator parody — works but is high-commitment branding
description: Landing hero copy: 'Hi, I'm Fix. I'm here to terminate your project.' followed by 'Building codes, materials lists, store prices, project planning — locked and loaded.' and 'I'll be back… with the receipts from Home Depot.' The Terminator references are clear and consistently executed across the landing page.
user_impact: As an expert I found this charming once I parsed it — the dark humor with construction work has a real fit (tool belts, drills, project completion). But the word 'terminate' could read negatively to anxious DIYers (different persona). For me it's a positive: shows the brand has a point of view, doesn't take itself too seriously.
expected: Branding is a judgment call — this works for confident users but may filter out cautious ones.
fix_recommendation: Keep the bot voice; it gives the product personality. Consider whether to soften for first-time visitors via an A/B test (the brand is a wildcard — keep it bold but watch beginner abandonment metrics).

title: AI structural-repair answer demonstrates expert-level technical depth and code literacy
description: For my question on sistering a cracked 2x10 joist (14ft span, 24in OC, tile floor), the AI returned a multi-section response with: correct IRC code citations (R502.1, R502.6, R502.7, R502.8, R301.5, Table R502.3.1(1)), NDS Supplement Table 11A reference, accurate bolt shear values (~400 lb single shear, ~700-750 double for 1/2in SPF), correct distinction between lag and through-bolt clamping, fastening pattern with end/edge distances (3.5in end, 4in spacing parallel to grain, 1.5D edge), and a candid comparison table on sister-only vs flitch-plate.
user_impact: This is genuinely the kind of response I'd hope for from a structural engineer doing a quick consult. The combination of code refs + practical numbers + 'when each option makes sense' is much better than the generic 'consult a professional' deflection I expect from AI tools. I would trust this enough to bring it to my AHJ.
expected: Expert-level depth without hand-holding — delivered.
fix_recommendation: Keep this. The system prompt and/or model is calibrated well for technical structural questions. Worth profiling what makes this work and applying to other domains.

title: AI follow-up correctly distinguishes drilled hole from notch under IRC R502.8
description: For my follow-up on drilling through a joist in a finished basement with 1-inch clearance: the AI correctly responded 'Legal — hole, not notch; R502.8 permits' and recommended a right-angle drill or flex-shaft for the physical clearance constraint. This is exactly the regulatory-vs-physical distinction I was testing for.
user_impact: As an expert this confirmed the AI understands the difference between a hole bored through a member (allowed within limits) and a notch (heavily restricted by R502.8 middle-third prohibition). That's a subtle but important code distinction many DIY resources get wrong.
fix_recommendation: Keep current model and prompt config — this level of code literacy is a differentiator.

title: 'Pre-contextualized answer' value prop on Talk-to-a-Pro page is strong
description: The /marketplace/qa Talk-to-a-Pro form had four bullet value-props: 'Pre-contextualized answer — your expert sees your full AI report, photos, and building codes upfront', 'Documented record — every answer stays with your project forever, not lost after a call', 'Payment protection — only charged when an expert claims; full refund if unanswered', 'Verified tradespeople — rated experts matched by specialty to your project'.
user_impact: This is the clearest value-prop articulation I've seen from a DIY-help product. Each bullet addresses a real pain point (re-explaining context, losing call notes, paying for unhelpful advice, finding qualified experts). Made me want to actually use the paid Q&A.
fix_recommendation: Promote these value-props higher — currently they're hidden behind the 'Talk to a Pro' tab toggle on landing. Make this visible on /about and on the chat surface where users might convert from AI to expert.

title: AppShell sidebar shows both DIY and EXPERT menus to dual-role users — may confuse
description: When signed in as test-expert-gc (an account that's both a DIYer and an Expert), the sidebar shows both 'DIY' (My Projects, My Tools, Shopping) and 'EXPERTS' (Find an Expert, My Questions) AND 'EXPERT' (Dashboard, Q&A Queue, AI Review Queue, Messages). That's 3 sections with 11+ links in a narrow sidebar.
user_impact: As an expert customer I had to scan past the EXPERT pro-side dashboard links to find the DIY chat I wanted. Information overload for dual-role users.
expected: Either (a) a top-of-sidebar role toggle (Customer Mode / Pro Mode), (b) collapse one section by default, or (c) merge DIY+EXPERTS into 'For Customers' and keep EXPERT as 'Pro Tools'.
fix_recommendation: Add a role toggle to AppSidebar at the top. Users should be able to switch context explicitly rather than always seeing both.

title: 'Save to Project' CTA appears after AI response — good filing affordance for experts
description: At the end of the AI chat response, a 'Save to Project' button appeared, allowing me to file the response into a project. This is exactly the kind of post-conversation action an expert wants — turn a one-shot question into a tracked reference.
user_impact: As an expert I bookmark and file references regularly. The 'Save to Project' CTA caught my attention as a power-user feature. I didn't click it (would have needed to be signed in), but the affordance is right.
fix_recommendation: Keep. Consider also adding 'Export as PDF' or 'Copy as Markdown' for technical references — experts often want to send these to clients or colleagues.

title: AI loses session context across page reloads — no obvious 'continue conversation' for guests
description: After my multi-turn structural-repair conversation, I navigated to /marketplace/qa to submit a paid question. When I came back to /, the chat input was reset to empty and the prior conversation appears lost (no obvious 'resume' link). This may be by design for guests (no persistence), but it's not communicated.
user_impact: As an expert I expected my conversation to persist in some form — at least via local storage for the current session. Losing the thread mid-evaluation made it harder to file a coherent follow-up paid question that referenced the AI's work.
expected: Either persist conversations in localStorage for guests, OR show a 'Sign in to save this conversation' prompt before allowing navigation away.
fix_recommendation: Add localStorage-backed conversation persistence for guest sessions. When a guest navigates back to /, restore the last conversation with a 'Continue' affordance or 'Start fresh' option.

title: Logged-in experts still see "AI-POWERED DIY ASSISTANT" landing hero
description: After logging in as a carpenter expert, the landing page (https://www.fixerator.com/) shows the same hero as anonymous users: 'AI-POWERED DIY ASSISTANT' eyebrow text, 'Hi, I'm Fix. I'm here to terminate your project.' headline, and DIYer-targeted suggestion chips (mortar/electrical/bathroom/deck-permits). A small 'Welcome back, <Pro name>' banner appears at the top but the entire main hero is unchanged.
user_impact: As a busy carpenter, my first impression is that this platform isn't built for me. The hero copy speaks to DIYers, not pros. I'd close the tab and write the platform off as 'consumer-side only' if I weren't here to test.
expected: Logged-in experts should see an expert-targeted hero: 'Welcome back, [Name] — N questions in your specialty, $X pending payouts, go to dashboard.' Or at minimum, a different headline/copy.
fix_recommendation: Differentiate landing hero by user role. Experts should land on or be redirected to their dashboard, or see a pro-specific hero treatment.

title: Dashboard 'Recent Questions' widget shows 43-day-old gibberish that 404s
description: On /experts/dashboard, the 'Recent Questions' widget surfaces a question titled 'sfsgdfs wfgseh;kamgh;msrhg;kmsg;kmsfg;,marf;msgdh;mawsfglndfglmn', tagged 'electrical', 'Free', '43d ago'. Clicking the link goes to /marketplace/qa/ca8caa00-06f0-42ad-a09e-ff9198e10714 which displays 'Question not found'.
user_impact: As a carpenter trying to evaluate the platform, the only content on my dashboard is gibberish that breaks. This screams 'low-quality platform' and is the first thing I see when I log in. I would not trust this with my time or banking details.
expected: Recent Questions widget should show real questions in my specialty, or an empty state like 'No recent questions in your specialty' if there are none. Stale/deleted questions should be filtered out.
fix_recommendation: Filter the Recent Questions widget by (a) expert's specialty and (b) question still exists/is open. Clean out test/seed data on production. Same bug noted in prior carpenter and electrician sweeps (2026-04-03) — it has persisted.

title: 'Hvac' capitalized incorrectly in Q&A queue filter chip
description: The Q&A queue filter chips read: All, Electrical, Plumbing, Hvac, Carpentry, Flooring, Roofing, Concrete, Drywall, Painting, Tile, Landscaping, General. 'Hvac' should be 'HVAC' (acronym for Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning). The Find an Expert specialty dropdown elsewhere correctly shows 'HVAC' — so this is inconsistent.
user_impact: As a tradesperson, this looks unprofessional. Trades have strict conventions for acronyms — 'Hvac' looks like a tech-bro built this without consulting an actual tradesperson.
expected: All instances should read 'HVAC' in caps. Match the Find an Expert dropdown which has it right.
fix_recommendation: Search for 'Hvac' in code (likely a string-case conversion). Force uppercase for HVAC, AC, MEP, NEC, IRC, UPC acronyms throughout.

title: Subscription tier feature lists duplicate platform fee line item
description: On /experts/dashboard/subscription, the Pro ($29/mo) tier lists '15% platform fee' twice (once as the headline fee, once in the feature checklist). Same bug in Premium ($79/mo): '12% platform fee' appears twice.
user_impact: As a carpenter doing my ROI math, the duplicate line item makes me think the page was rushed or the feature set is thin. It also looks unprofessional next to the platform fee headline.
expected: Either remove 'X% platform fee' from the checklist (it's already in the headline) or replace those lines with another concrete benefit.
fix_recommendation: Remove the duplicate '15% platform fee' / '12% platform fee' lines from the feature checklists, or replace with specific benefits like 'Up to N free question reposts' or 'Profile pinned to top of trade searches'.

title: No license/certification/insurance fields on carpenter profile
description: Viewing 'Mike the Carpenter' public profile (/experts/140f2e0f-...), the displayed fields are: name, rating, location, hourly rate, Q&A rate, bio, specialties (Carpentry · 15 yrs), CTAs. There is no place to display: contractor license number, OSHA certification, insurance/bonding, sub-trade specialties (framing vs trim vs cabinetry), portfolio photos, references, or union affiliation.
user_impact: As a carpenter competing against generic 'Slizzy Industry' and 'Willy's Welding' test profiles, I have no way to prove I'm legit. A DIYer choosing between $75/hr carpenters sees only hours, rating (always 0), and location. That's not enough trust signal to convert.
expected: Profile should let me showcase: license #, state, insurance status, bond status, sub-specialty tags, portfolio gallery, and verifiable credentials. Verified badges should appear next to validated items.
fix_recommendation: Add credential and portfolio fields to the expert profile model. Add a 'Verified License' badge after admin verification. Show sub-trade tags as separate chips (e.g., 'Framing', 'Trim', 'Cabinetry').

title: Test data leak: 'Willy's Welding — Your Mom, CA' visible on production
description: On https://www.fixerator.com/experts, the public expert directory lists 'Willy's Welding' located in 'Your Mom, CA' with $75/hr and specialty tags 'other / drywall / roofing'. This is obvious test data that's leaked to production.
user_impact: As a real carpenter evaluating whether to register on this platform, seeing 'Your Mom, CA' as a location makes the platform look amateurish. If I were a DIYer, I wouldn't trust other experts on this directory either.
expected: Test accounts should be filtered out of the public expert directory, or at minimum tagged as 'Demo' / hidden behind a query param.
fix_recommendation: Add an is_test_account flag (or specific email-domain filter for @diyhelper.test) on the experts list query in /experts (Find an Expert) page.

title: All experts in directory show identical $75/hr — suggests rates not editable
description: On /experts, all 8 listed experts (Slizzy Industry, Mike Thompson, Sarah the Electrician, Dave the Plumber, Lisa the HVAC Tech, Tony the General Contractor, Willy's Welding, Mike the Carpenter) display '$75/hr' as the hourly rate. Mike Thompson has no rate displayed at all. All show '(0)' ratings.
user_impact: As a carpenter who knows rates vary by trade and region (a master finish carpenter charges differently than a deck builder), seeing all $75/hr makes me think the platform doesn't let me set my own rate, or that the test seed only used $75. Either way, no rate diversity = no real marketplace.
expected: Rates should vary between experts, reflecting market reality. Seed data should reflect that.
fix_recommendation: Vary seed account rates across trades. Verify that rate-setting actually persists and updates the public profile.

title: Public 'Become an Expert' page missing tradesperson-specific trust signals
description: On /experts/register (when logged out), the page shows 3 benefit cards (Answer & Earn, Build Your Rep, Set Your Terms) and a 3-step How It Works. No mention of: typical earnings range, platform fee, payout method/frequency (Stripe Connect), verification timeline, testimonials from real experts, or trade-specific example questions.
user_impact: As a carpenter visiting cold, I need to know: 'How much can I actually earn?' 'What's the cut?' 'Will I get paid weekly?' 'How long does verification take?' Without these answers, I'd close the tab. My time is money — I won't sign up to find out.
expected: Add: earnings range estimates, platform fees up front (18%/15%/12% depending on tier), 'Get paid via Stripe Connect — direct deposit weekly', 'Verification typically takes 24-48 hours', 1-3 real expert testimonials with photo and earnings, and 2-3 trade-specific example questions.
fix_recommendation: Add an 'Earnings & Fees' section above 'How It Works'. Add a testimonial carousel. Add trade-specific example question chips.

title: AI Review Queue — no payout shown for expert corrections
description: On /experts/dashboard/reviews, the description reads: 'Borderline AI responses in your specialty areas. Submit corrections or confirm they look good.' Nowhere does it say whether I get paid for submitting a correction, or get reputation credit, or anything.
user_impact: As a carpenter, why would I spend my time reviewing AI responses for free? My time is billable. If the platform wants me to QA the AI's carpentry advice, I need a clear value exchange.
expected: State the incentive: 'Earn $X per validated correction' or 'Earn reputation points that boost your search ranking' or 'Get a credit toward next subscription month'. If it's purely altruistic, say that explicitly.
fix_recommendation: Add an incentive label to the AI Review Queue page header. If you don't have payouts wired yet, label it as 'Volunteer reviews (beta — payout structure TBD)' to set expectations honestly.

title: Deep expert pages occasionally redirect to /?signIn=true despite valid session
description: Navigated to /experts/dashboard/subscription while logged in as an expert. Got redirected to /?signIn=true (sign-in screen) even though session was valid (the 'Welcome back, <Pro>' banner at top still rendered, indicating server knew I was logged in). Subsequent navigation to other pages worked. Concurrent agent session contention may be aggravating this, but the inconsistency between top-banner state (logged in) and page-level guard (not logged in) is a real bug.
user_impact: As a carpenter clicking through my own dashboard, getting bounced to the sign-in page mid-flow makes me think the platform is buggy. Compounding: there is NO sign-in modal popping up — I have to figure out how to sign in again.
expected: If session is valid (as evidenced by header state), expert pages should load. If session expired, show a clear 'Session expired, please sign in' message and trigger the sign-in modal automatically.
fix_recommendation: Audit the auth guard on /experts/dashboard/* routes — likely a race condition between cookie validation and SSR. Show a sign-in modal automatically when redirected, instead of dropping the user on landing.

title: Account menu has no link to Expert Dashboard
description: After clicking the user account dropdown (top-right of sidebar bottom), the menu shows: My Profile, Settings, Messages, Sign Out. There's no 'Expert Dashboard' or 'Q&A Queue' shortcut. For an expert user, the most important link in the user dropdown should be 'Expert Dashboard'.
user_impact: As a carpenter, my dropdown should make it one-click to get to my earnings/queue. Instead I have to use the sidebar nav.
expected: Add 'Expert Dashboard' as the first menu item when user has expert role.
fix_recommendation: Conditionally show 'Expert Dashboard' menuitem at top of account dropdown for users with expert role.

title: Sidebar nav loses Expert section after closing 'My Questions' overlay
description: On the /experts/dashboard page, clicking 'My Questions' opens an overlay panel. Closing the overlay (X button) caused the sidebar to lose its full nav state — only 'FIXERATOR' and 'Sign In' button rendered, instead of the full DIY/EXPERTS/EXPERT nav. Refreshing fixed it.
user_impact: As a carpenter, the sidebar disappearing is jarring. Looks like the site broke. Not blocking but unprofessional.
expected: Closing the overlay should not affect sidebar state. Sidebar should remain visible with full nav.
fix_recommendation: Investigate the My Questions overlay component — likely a state-management bug where overlay close incorrectly toggles sidebar visibility.

title: Q&A Queue is empty across all trade filters — chicken-and-egg marketplace
description: On /experts/dashboard/qa, the queue shows '0 available' under ALL filter chips (All, Electrical, Plumbing, Hvac, Carpentry, Flooring, Roofing, Concrete, Drywall, Painting, Tile, Landscaping, General). Filter chip selection (Carpentry) visually highlights but doesn't add content.
user_impact: As a carpenter who came to the platform to earn money answering questions, there's nothing for me to do. Empty marketplace = no revenue = I close the tab and never come back. This is the most critical retention problem for experts.
expected: At least some seed questions per trade so experts can evaluate the experience. Or a clearer 'beta — we're seeding the queue' callout.
fix_recommendation: Seed at least 3-5 real-looking questions per trade. Or paid promo questions ('Free $5 for the first answer in your trade'). Address the supply/demand chicken-and-egg explicitly in the dashboard empty state.

title: Subscription tier — 18% Free platform fee is steep, no math to justify upgrade
description: On /experts/dashboard/subscription: Free = 18% fee, Pro $29/mo = 15% fee (saves 3%), Premium $79/mo = 12% fee (saves 6% total). To break even on Pro, I need $29/0.03 = $967/mo in Q&A revenue. To break even on Premium, $79/0.06 = $1,317/mo. No earnings calculator, no avg earnings shown, no '47 carpenters earned over $1,000 last month' social proof.
user_impact: As a carpenter, I see 18% as a steep cut on a $15 Q&A payout — that's $2.70 the platform takes. To justify $29/mo Pro, I need to be answering ~16+ questions per month. I have no idea if that's realistic.
expected: Show me earnings examples, an earnings calculator, or 'Last month, Pro carpenters earned an avg of $X' so I can decide which tier matches my expected volume.
fix_recommendation: Add an interactive ROI calculator: 'If you answer N questions/mo at $X/question, Pro saves you $Y vs Free'. Show median expert earnings per tier.

title: Fixerator brand voice resonates with tradesperson identity
description: The mascot tagline 'Hi, I'm Fix. I'm here to terminate your project.' followed by 'I'll be back… with the receipts from Home Depot.' lands well for a tradesperson persona. 'Terminate' = finish a project (in trades lingo, 'closing out' or 'wrapping up'). The Terminator-bot mascot reads as a job-site buddy, not a polite consumer app.
user_impact: As a carpenter, this is the first DIY platform that doesn't feel like it was built by tech-bros pretending to know construction. The voice is playful but knows its audience. Made me actually want to keep exploring.
expected: Keep this voice across the expert side too — currently the expert dashboard is bland and bureaucratic ('Dashboard / Bronze expert · Free / standard queue').
fix_recommendation: Carry the Fixerator/'Fix' brand voice into the expert dashboard. E.g., 'You've terminated 0 projects this month — let's fix that.' (with empty state).

title: 'Charge on Claim' policy clearly stated on Q&A Queue
description: The Q&A Queue page prominently states: 'Charge on Claim — When you claim a question, the DIYer's card is charged and you have 2 hours to answer. If you don't answer in time, they're automatically refunded.' This is a tradesperson-friendly, clear policy that sets expectations on both sides.
user_impact: As a carpenter calculating risk, knowing I have exactly 2 hours after claiming to answer (or DIYer gets refunded) is super clear. No ambiguity about whether I'll get paid.
expected: Keep this clarity. Maybe expand to: 'Your payout for this question: $X. Platform fee: $Y. You receive: $Z.' before claiming.
fix_recommendation: On a claimable question card, show net payout to the expert. Pro/Premium tiers should show the platform-fee delta as social proof for upgrading.

title: AI carpentry response (deck framing) is technically excellent
description: Tested chat with: 'I'm building a 12x16 deck attached to a single-story house ledger. What size joists, joist spacing, and ledger fastener pattern do I need for a 40 psf live load? In Massachusetts.' Response correctly cited: MA adopting IRC + 780 CMR, IRC Table R507.6 joist span values (2x8 13'-1" @ 12 O.C., etc. — all match IRC 2018), correct lag screw row spacing (2" edge, ⅓ and ⅔ pattern, 1½" penetration past rim), LedgerLOK code approval via TER 1203-03, Simpson DTT2Z lateral ties, Simpson LUS210 joist hangers, proper HDG/SS fastener corrosion requirements, MA permit requirement. Nuanced call-out that 2x8 @ 16 O.C. is borderline at 11'-10" max for 12' span. Recommended professional verification for safety-critical ledger attachment with a CTA to 'Ask a verified expert →'.
user_impact: As a carpenter, this is the kind of answer I'd give a customer. Technically accurate, code-cited, safety-aware, with specific Simpson part numbers. The AI doesn't overreach — it explicitly recommends professional verification for safety-critical work. This makes me NOT worried that the AI is making me look bad if a DIYer follows it.
expected: Continue this quality. Maybe also include footing/post-base hardware (Simpson ABU/AB66Z) and beam sizing.
fix_recommendation: Expand carpentry prompts to include footings, beams, and post-base hardware by default — those are common follow-ups.

title: AI response includes lead-generation hook to verified experts
description: After the technical deck-framing answer, the AI includes a callout: 'Want expert confirmation? Ledger attachment and flashing details are safety-critical and frequently cited on MA deck inspections — a verified structural consultant or licensed contractor can review your specific house rim board condition before you commit to a fastener pattern. Ask a verified expert →' (links to /marketplace/qa).
user_impact: As a carpenter who came here to earn money, this is the lead-gen path I need. The AI doesn't try to do my job — it acknowledges its limits and routes high-value, safety-critical work to verified experts (me). That's the platform business model working correctly.
expected: Make sure these expert-routing CTAs surface on every safety-critical response. Also: when an unauthenticated DIYer clicks 'Ask a verified expert →', they currently land on /?signIn=true with no context — should preserve the AI conversation context across the sign-in flow.
fix_recommendation: Preserve chat context when clicking 'Ask a verified expert' — pass conversationId in URL or session, so DIYer doesn't have to re-explain after sign-in.

title: Public expert directory pill capitalization inconsistent
description: On /experts (Find an Expert), specialty pills on expert cards show 'carpentry', 'electrical', 'plumbing', 'hvac', 'general contracting' — all lowercase. But the specialty filter dropdown shows 'Carpentry', 'Electrical', 'HVAC', 'General Contracting' — Title Case with HVAC capitalized.
user_impact: As a carpenter, the mismatched capitalization is small but reads as inconsistent — and as a pro, the lowercase pills feel less professional than the dropdown labels.
expected: Match capitalization between specialty pills and filter dropdown. Pro convention: Title Case with HVAC/AC/MEP/NEC etc as full caps.
fix_recommendation: Use a single specialty-label source-of-truth (a TRADE_LABELS const) and apply consistently in pills and dropdowns.

title: Stripe Connect setup banner present but no info on payout schedule
description: On /experts/dashboard, a yellow warning banner reads: 'Complete Stripe Setup — Set up your Stripe account to receive payments from answered questions.' with a 'Complete Setup' button. However, nowhere on the dashboard or the subscription page is there info on: payout schedule (daily/weekly/monthly?), minimum payout amount, supported countries, or Stripe Connect fees.
user_impact: As a carpenter, I'm being asked to enter bank details for Stripe before I can earn anything. I need to know HOW and WHEN I get paid before I trust the platform with that info. Stripe Connect has its own fees on top of platform's 18%. No transparency = no trust.
expected: Before clicking 'Complete Setup', show: 'Payouts via Stripe Connect — direct deposit weekly, no minimum, US/CA supported, no additional Stripe fees (covered by platform)' — or whatever the actual terms are.
fix_recommendation: Add a 'Payout Details' info card next to the Stripe Setup banner that surfaces all relevant payout terms BEFORE the user clicks into Stripe's flow.

title: 'I'm here to terminate your project' mascot copy is tone-deaf for HVAC emergencies
description: Landing hero reads 'Hi, I'm Fix. I'm here to terminate your project.' For HVAC searchers, the most urgent traffic comes from no-heat-in-January or no-A/C-in-July emergencies — often life-safety scenarios involving CO risk, frozen pipes, or heat stroke for elderly occupants. The word 'terminate' (Terminator brand voice) reads as destructive, not protective. A homeowner with a short-cycling furnace and a CO concern is not in a campy-action-movie mood.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech evaluating whether to bring my emergency-call customers to this platform, the landing copy made me hesitant. If grandma's furnace just locked out on a -10F night and she lands on 'I'm here to terminate your project', she's clicking back. I lose a lead.
expected: Reassuring, capable copy that matches the seriousness of trade work. 'Hi, I'm Fix — I'll help you finish the job' or 'Hi, I'm Fix — Let's get this fixed' would still keep brand voice without the destructive verb.
fix_recommendation: A/B test the hero copy. Consider trade-context variants: HVAC/electrical/plumbing visitors arriving via search likely have urgent problems; carpenter/general visitors may be projects-in-planning. Or soften 'terminate' to 'finish' / 'crush' / 'get this done'.

title: AI Review Queue sidebar link returns 404 at /experts/dashboard/ai-review
description: The expert sidebar shows 'AI Review Queue' as a primary nav item under the EXPERT section. Clicking it (or navigating directly to /experts/dashboard/ai-review) returns Next.js 404 'This page could not be found.' This is a broken navigation entry exposed in the live sidebar to every authenticated expert.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech orienting myself in my first session, I clicked AI Review Queue expecting a queue of AI-generated answers to verify for payout. Instead I hit a 404 and lost trust in the platform's polish. If they can't keep their own sidebar links wired up, can I trust them with my Stripe payouts?
expected: AI Review Queue link should land on a functioning queue page, OR be hidden from the sidebar until the feature is live.
fix_recommendation: Either implement the /experts/dashboard/ai-review route (the sidebar implies it should exist) or remove the link from AppSidebar until the page ships. Likely lives in components/AppSidebar.tsx and the matching app/experts/dashboard/ai-review/page.tsx is missing.

title: /login route returns 404 — sign-in is modal-only with no fallback URL
description: Navigating directly to https://fixerator.com/login (a common bookmark/typed URL) returns a 404 page. The actual sign-in experience is a modal triggered from a sidebar 'Sign In' button. Auth redirects also reference /login internally in some flows.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech who bookmarked the login page on my phone for quick access between jobs, I hit a 404 every time. I almost gave up assuming the platform was broken before noticing the tiny Sign In button in the bottom-left sidebar.
expected: /login should either render the sign-in modal/page, or 302 redirect to /?signIn=true (the modal trigger URL).
fix_recommendation: Add app/login/page.tsx that either renders a server-side sign-in form or redirects to /?signIn=true. The redirect pattern already exists in some links to that query param.

title: Q&A Queue has only 1 ancient (43-day-old) gibberish question across ALL trades
description: The /experts/dashboard/qa page shows 'Open Questions: 1 available' total, and that one question is 'sfsgdfs wfgseh;kamgh;msrhg;kmsg;kmsfg;,marf;msgdh;mawsfglndfglmn' — keyboard-mash test data — tagged 'electrical' and '43d ago'. Filtering by 'Hvac' yields 'No questions match your filter'. This means the entire HVAC expert side of the marketplace has zero earnable inventory.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech who logged in to evaluate the platform, my Q&A queue had zero relevant questions. There is no revenue opportunity here. Even if I subscribe to Pro ($29/mo) for priority queue access, priority over zero questions is still zero. I'd close my account by end of week.
expected: Either seed the production marketplace with real questions across all 12 specialties (or at least a couple per specialty), or clearly explain to experts on Day 1 that the platform is pre-launch and questions will start flowing on date X.
fix_recommendation: Two paths: (a) Delete the gibberish '43d ago' question from production — it's user data exposure of someone's keyboard mash. (b) Seed the QA queue with a handful of realistic questions per trade so new experts see the platform working. (c) Show empty-state copy that sets expectations ('Q&A volume is ramping up — typical wait time: X days') rather than just '0 available'.

title: Specialty filter chip displays 'Hvac' (title-case) — should be 'HVAC' (acronym)
description: On /experts/dashboard/qa the filter row shows: All, Electrical, Plumbing, Hvac, Carpentry, Flooring, etc. 'Hvac' is universally written as the acronym 'HVAC' in the trade (Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning). 'Hvac' reads as if a non-tradesperson wrote the UI. The same casing problem appears in the Specialties selector on the expert profile (HVAC is correct there) and on the Find an Expert specialty dropdown (HVAC is correct there). Only the Q&A Queue filter row is wrong.
user_impact: As an HVAC pro, the moment I see 'Hvac' on a screen I assume the platform was built by someone outside the trades. It's a small thing that signals lack of domain credibility. I'd be skeptical of every other piece of advice on the platform.
expected: 'HVAC' (all caps) everywhere it appears, matching how the rest of the app already labels it.
fix_recommendation: Single source of truth for specialty labels. The Q&A queue filter is title-casing a value that should be ALL_CAPS for HVAC. Likely in the Q&A filter component — capitalize(specialty.toLowerCase()) is the wrong transform for HVAC. Use a label map: { hvac: 'HVAC', electrical: 'Electrical', ... }.

title: Expert profile has no EPA 608 / NATE / state HVAC license fields
description: The expert profile at /experts/dashboard/profile has generic License Type/Number/State fields with placeholders 'e.g., General Contractor' and 'e.g., GC-12345' that don't fit HVAC credentials. There is no EPA Section 608 certification field (federally REQUIRED for any tech handling refrigerant — handling R-410A or R-32 without it is a violation), no NATE certification, no HVAC Excellence cert, no brand-specific certification (Mitsubishi Diamond, Daikin Comfort Pro, etc.). For DIYers asking refrigerant questions, EPA 608 is the single most important credential signal.
user_impact: As a licensed HVAC tech with EPA 608 Universal, NATE certs, and a state Mechanical License, I cannot display any of that on my profile. The DIYer asking a refrigerant question can't tell if I'm certified to even legally answer. I'd assume my expertise isn't valued here.
expected: HVAC-specific credential fields: EPA 608 (Type I/II/III/Universal selector), NATE certification, state HVAC/Mechanical license type with state-specific dropdown of license classes. Maybe a 'verified' badge after upload of credential photo.
fix_recommendation: Make the Credentials section dynamic based on primary specialty. If specialty includes 'HVAC', show EPA 608 type and NATE fields. If electrical, show NEC certification etc. The 'placeholder' approach (e.g., General Contractor) only serves the GC use case.

title: Subscription tier card lists '15% platform fee' twice (header + bullet) for Pro tier
description: On /experts/dashboard/subscription, the Pro tier card shows '$29/mo' header, then '15% platform fee' as a subhead, then bullets: Priority queue, Analytics dashboard, '15% platform fee'. The platform fee appears twice — once as a tier-level attribute and again as a feature bullet. Same on Premium ('12% platform fee' duplicated). Free tier doesn't have this duplication.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech doing back-of-envelope ROI math on whether $29/mo is worth saving 3% in fees, the duplicate listing made me re-read the cards three times trying to find what was actually different about Pro vs Free. Wasted cognitive effort.
expected: Each tier lists its fee once, then unique bullet features.
fix_recommendation: Remove the duplicate fee line from the bullets list. Likely in app/experts/dashboard/subscription/page.tsx or wherever the tier feature arrays live.

title: Subscription tier value proposition can't be evaluated with empty queue
description: With zero HVAC questions in the queue, every subscription tier benefit (Priority queue, Direct question routing, Featured profile, Project leads) is gated on inventory I cannot see today. Break-even math: Pro saves 3% in fees, so I need to earn $29/0.03 = $967/mo in answered questions to break even. With zero questions available, all paid tiers are negative-ROI for HVAC pros.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech evaluating Pro/Premium upgrades, the tier comparison page is functionally meaningless. The benefits would only matter if I could see expected question volume in my trade. Right now I'd never upgrade — the math doesn't work.
expected: Each tier card shows historical-or-projected question volume for my specialty, expected earnings ranges, and time-to-break-even. Or a 'Pro is best for experts answering 5+ questions/week' soft guidance.
fix_recommendation: Add a 'For HVAC experts: avg 3 questions/week, $20-40 each, break-even = X weeks' callout per tier. Or hold off on prompting upgrades until question volume in the user's specialty justifies it.

title: Sidebar account name doesn't update after navigation — stale user identity
description: After signing in as test-expert-hvac, the sidebar bottom-left intermittently displayed 'test-expert-carpenter', 'test-diyer-intermediate', 'Sarah the Electrician', and 'Tony the General Contractor' — depending on what page was loaded and how recently other tabs had refreshed. Verified the page content (dashboard, profile fields) often DID match the displayed sidebar name, so it's not pure caching: the auth session itself appears to leak across tabs in the same Chrome profile.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech using the platform on my work iPad (single Chrome profile), if my partner logs in on another tab my session could swap mid-task. Privacy nightmare. Imagine submitting an answer under the wrong expert account.
expected: Each tab maintains its own authenticated session, or session changes propagate everywhere atomically. The currently logged-in user name in the sidebar should always match the cookies being sent on requests.
fix_recommendation: This may be a Supabase auth session storage issue (localStorage shared across tabs in same origin). Confirm whether multiple BrowserClients across tabs are racing on session refresh. May require tab-scoped session keys or BroadcastChannel sync. Note: this was observed during a parallel-agent test where multiple agents shared a Chrome profile — the bug may not reproduce in single-user use, but if any platform user has multiple Fixerator tabs open it could still trigger.

title: 'Find an Expert' lists Slizzy Industry / Willy's Welding alongside test accounts — test data leaking to prod directory
description: On /experts the public directory shows a mix of clearly-test accounts ('Lisa the HVAC Tech', 'Tony the General Contractor', 'Mike Thompson') alongside more-realistic-but-still-suspect entries ('Slizzy Industry — Portsmouth NH', 'Willy's Welding — Your Mom, CA'). The 'Your Mom, CA' city is profane test data exposed to the public expert directory.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech evaluating whether real DIYers use this platform, the directory looks like a beta test environment. Real homeowners landing on Find-an-Expert and seeing 'Your Mom, CA' as a city would not take the platform seriously.
expected: Either hide test accounts from the public expert directory, or scrub clearly-fake content like 'Your Mom, CA' city values.
fix_recommendation: Add an is_test_account flag to expert profiles and filter from the public /experts directory. Or, at minimum, run a script to scrub obvious profanity/joke values from the production experts table.

title: Logged-out sidebar collapses to empty rail — Become-an-Expert CTA hidden
description: When signed out, the entire sidebar (DIY section, EXPERTS section, EXPERT section) disappears and the user sees only the FIXERATOR wordmark at top-left and a tiny 'Sign In' button at bottom-left. The 'Become an Expert' link is only available in the footer on the chat page. For a logged-out HVAC tech who heard about the platform from a buddy, there's no clear path-to-expert-registration in the primary navigation.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech curious about earning extra income on the platform, I had no obvious 'Become an Expert' CTA when I first landed. I had to scroll to the footer of the chat to find it. A busy tradesperson would bounce.
expected: Logged-out users should see a Become-an-Expert or 'Are you a pro?' CTA prominently — either in the sidebar, hero, or both. Currently the sidebar is wasted real estate when logged out.
fix_recommendation: Add a sidebar section for unauthenticated users with 'About', 'Become an Expert', 'Find an Expert' links. Or surface Become-an-Expert as a secondary CTA next to 'Talk to a Pro' in the chat hero.

title: Expert banner correctly shows specialty-filtered open question count
description: When logged in as an electrician (via session leak from another agent), the top welcome banner showed 'Welcome back, Sarah the Electrician — 1 open question'. The single question in the queue was tagged 'electrical', so the count is properly filtered to the expert's specialty rather than showing total marketplace volume. This is exactly the right UX — experts care about THEIR queue, not the global one.
user_impact: If I were logged in as an HVAC expert and there were 50 questions in the marketplace but none HVAC, I'd want to see '0 open questions' not '50'. The specialty filter respects my time.
expected: What I observed — specialty-filtered count.
fix_recommendation: Keep this behavior. Add a 'recommended for you' or 'open questions in your area' as the secondary metric if cross-specialty discovery is desired.

title: AI mini-split sizing response is HVAC-pro-quality — Manual J, code-correct, MN-specific
description: Tested AI chat with a real Manual-J-style question: sizing a mini-split for a 400 sq ft three-season room in Minnesota with R-21/R-50/R-10 envelope. Response delivered: correct cooling/heating load breakdown by component (~6,700 BTU cooling, ~6,600 heating); 9K BTU recommendation with explicit warning against oversizing (correct — short-cycling and poor latent removal); named real cold-climate hyper-heat models (Mitsubishi MSZ-FH09, Fujitsu, Daikin); correct electrical (240V 15-20A dedicated, outdoor disconnect in sight); cited MN Mechanical Code Chapter 1346 correctly; named real utility rebate programs (Xcel Energy, CenterPoint, MN Power); flagged slab edge insulation and air-sealing-before-sizing — both pro-level callouts.
user_impact: As an HVAC pro, I'd actually use this output as a starting point for a customer conversation. The Manual J table is the right approach, the model selection is current, and the MN code/rebate localization shows the AI did the work I'd expect of a junior tech.
expected: What I observed.
fix_recommendation: Keep doing this. Two minor nits a senior pro would flag: (1) south window solar gain coefficient of 150 BTU/sf is on the high end — typical low-e double-pane is 100-130. (2) The claim that 'most 9K units are rated to -13F to -22F' is true only for hyper-heat specific models; standard 9K mini-splits lose 50%+ capacity below 17F. Worth a one-line qualifier.

title: AI furnace short-cycling response correctly escalates CO risk to expert referral
description: Tested AI chat with a furnace short-cycling question on a 15-year-old 90% AFUE unit. Response correctly: identified 90-second-runtime-then-shutdown as classic safety lockout pattern; ordered diagnostic steps properly (read LED flash code FIRST, then filter, then flame sensor, then condensate); correctly identified flame sensor oxidation as #1 cause on 15-yr units; safe-DIY steps for flame sensor cleaning with steel wool/emery cloth; condensate drain check specific to 90% AFUE float switch; cost estimates accurate ($10-25 flame sensor, $150-400 inducer, $200-600 control board). CRITICALLY: the response prominently flagged 'cracked heat exchanger on a 15-year-old furnace is a carbon monoxide risk and not something to DIY' and routed to an expert via the 'Ask a verified expert' link. This is exactly the right escalation behavior for life-safety scenarios.
user_impact: As an HVAC pro, this is the kind of triage I'd want from a referring channel. The AI does enough to help DIYers identify trivial fixes (flame sensor, filter) without letting them poke at heat exchangers and gas valves. The CO callout is the right risk profile for the unit age.
expected: Continued safety-first triage with prominent escalation to humans for combustion/gas safety scenarios.
fix_recommendation: Add tracking on the 'Ask a verified expert' link click-through rate from these safety-callout boxes — that's the most valuable lead-gen path for HVAC pros and should be measured/optimized.

title: Dark theme works visually but Hi/I'm Fix mascot crop appears cut off on right edge
description: On the landing hero in dark mode, the mascot 'Fix' robot appears with arms/tools extending to the right edge of the visible area, and one arm/wrench appears clipped at the right. The rest of the dark design is clean — good contrast on body copy, gold-on-dark accent text reads well, sidebar nav has adequate hit targets.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech browsing on a 13-inch laptop in a truck cab, the cropped mascot looks like a layout bug rather than intentional. A tradesperson with one bar of LTE would just assume the page didn't fully load.
expected: Mascot fully contained within the hero panel at standard laptop widths (1280-1440px).
fix_recommendation: Inspect the hero image positioning at 1280px width. Likely an absolute-positioned mascot needs max-width or right-margin adjustment.

title: No Stripe Connect onboarding clarity — banner says 'Complete Setup' with no fee/timing breakdown
description: Dashboard shows 'Complete Stripe Setup — Set up your Stripe account to receive payments from answered questions' with a 'Complete Setup' button. Nothing on the dashboard explains: (1) what the platform fee is on payouts (it's 18% per the subscription tier but that's a different page); (2) when payouts hit my bank account (next-day? weekly?); (3) what info Stripe needs from me (SSN, bank routing, etc.); (4) what happens if I skip Stripe setup.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech wary of giving bank details to a new platform, I want to know the payout terms BEFORE I start filling out Stripe Connect. The current 'Complete Setup' button is a black-box CTA. I'd procrastinate or never finish onboarding, which kills my ability to earn on the platform.
expected: A pre-Stripe explainer: 'You'll earn X% of each question (Y% platform fee). Payouts hit your bank 2 business days after a DIYer accepts your answer. Stripe will ask for: SSN (for 1099 tax reporting), bank routing/account. This takes ~5 minutes.'
fix_recommendation: Add an explainer modal or expandable panel before the 'Complete Setup' click. Or replace 'Complete Setup' with 'Set up payouts (5 min) →' and link to a brief intro page that covers fees, timing, and required info.

title: Mascot tagline 'I'll be back… with the receipts from Home Depot' is well-executed brand voice
description: The secondary tagline pill 'I'll be back… with the receipts from Home Depot' (Terminator reference, mono font) is actually solid brand voice. It's playful, references the construction/DIY context (Home Depot receipts), and reads as in-on-the-joke rather than aggressive. The 'terminate your project' headline doesn't land but THIS secondary line does.
user_impact: As an HVAC tech, I noticed the receipt callout and smiled. It's the kind of joke a tradesperson would tell. It made me want to read more.
expected: Continue this style of brand voice — references that ring true to tradespeople.
fix_recommendation: Lean MORE into receipt/job-site humor in secondary copy and LESS into the 'terminate' destructive verb in primary copy. The vibe of the secondary line is the win.

title: Q&A submit fails with raw zod enum error on default 'General' category
description: On the Q&A submission form (/marketplace/qa?targetExpertId=...), the Category dropdown defaults to 'General'. Submitting with that default returns a raw red error: 'Invalid option: expected one of "electrical"|"plumbing"|"hvac"|"carpentry"|"flooring"|"roofing"|"concrete"|"drywall"|"painting"|"tile"|"landscaping"|"general_contracting"|"other"'. Changing to 'Plumbing' lets submission succeed. The UI option 'General' maps to a value (presumably 'general') that the backend zod schema does not accept.
user_impact: On my very first paid question to a verified expert, the form crashed with internal validator language. I would not know that I need to pick a category — the form already had one selected. As an intermediate, I either retry blindly or bounce.
expected: Either the default category should be valid, or the validation message should be user-friendly ('Please select a category that matches your trade')
fix_recommendation: Remove 'General' from UI or map it to backend 'other'. Replace raw zod error with friendly copy. Files: likely src/app/marketplace/qa/ form component and the question-create API route.

title: Expert 'Welcome back, [name]' banner shows stale/wrong name and persists after signout
description: The orange/copper banner across expert-side and consumer-side pages (/experts, /experts/:id, /marketplace/qa) renders 'Welcome back, [name]' but the name shown does not match the actual authenticated user shown in the sidebar bottom account pill. Observed names like 'Welcome back, Tony the General Contractor' while sidebar showed 'Sarah the Electrician'. After signing out completely (Sign In button visible bottom-left), the banner STILL displayed 'Welcome back, Tony the General Contractor'.
user_impact: I have no idea what account I am in or whether I am even logged in. As an intermediate user navigating a multi-tab session this is disorienting and trust-eroding.
expected: Banner should always reflect current authenticated user, or hide entirely when signed out.
fix_recommendation: Tie banner state to live session (probably useSession hook), not cached state. Hide on signout. Likely component: ExpertWelcomeBanner or AppShell layout.

title: Expert welcome banner shown to consumer-only users / on consumer pages
description: The orange 'Welcome back, [expert]... 0 open questions... Go to Dashboard' banner appears on consumer-facing pages (Find an Expert, Q&A submission, marketplace question detail) even when the user is browsing as a consumer. This is the expert-side welcome bar bleeding into consumer flows.
user_impact: As a consumer DIYer (intermediate), I do not have an expert dashboard. The banner is irrelevant chrome telling me to 'Go to Dashboard' I do not own. Confused about which mode I am in.
expected: Banner should only appear when user has expert role AND on relevant pages. Hide entirely for consumer-only sessions.
fix_recommendation: Conditional render: only show ExpertWelcomeBanner when session.user.roles includes 'expert' AND not on /experts/dashboard already.

title: Routes /projects, /shopping, /login all return 404
description: Direct URL navigation to /projects, /shopping, /login each return a 404 'This page could not be found.' even though sidebar nav has 'My Projects', 'Shopping', and the app has a login modal. These look like intended routes a power user would deep-link.
user_impact: As an intermediate I deep-link by habit. Three 404s in five minutes destroys my trust that the product is finished.
expected: At minimum, /login should exist or redirect to /?signIn=true. /projects and /shopping should redirect to / with the relevant drawer pre-opened.
fix_recommendation: Add Next.js route handlers (or middleware redirects) for /login -> /?signIn=true, /projects -> /?drawer=projects, /shopping -> /?drawer=shopping. Or create real pages mirroring the drawer content.

title: Sidebar drawer items ('My Projects', 'My Tools') have no affordance distinguishing them from nav links
description: Sidebar items 'My Projects' and 'My Tools' open in-sidebar drawer panels rather than navigating. Items like 'Find an Expert', 'My Questions', 'Dashboard' navigate to pages. Visually they look identical — no caret, no panel-expand icon, no distinct hover state.
user_impact: I clicked thinking I'd navigate, was confused when a drawer opened next to the sidebar instead. Worse, when on a 404 page, clicking the drawer left the 404 visible in the main area, looking even more broken.
expected: Visual affordance (small panel-icon on right) showing 'this opens a drawer'. Drawer open should also redirect main area to a safe landing.
fix_recommendation: Add chevron-right icon to drawer-type sidebar items. On drawer open from a 404/error page, navigate main area to /.

title: 'General' category label is misleading — collides with 'general_contracting'
description: Q&A category dropdown shows 'General' as an option. But the backend taxonomy uses 'general_contracting' (the trade) and 'other' (catch-all). 'General' in the UI either maps incorrectly or is ambiguous.
user_impact: As an intermediate I selected 'General' assuming it meant catch-all home improvement, not general contracting. Confused taxonomy makes me less confident I picked the right route.
expected: Either rename to 'Other / Not Sure' for catch-all, OR rename to 'General Contracting' if it means the trade.
fix_recommendation: Decide: is 'General' = 'other' (catch-all) or = 'general_contracting' (trade)? Update label and ensure dropdown values match backend enum.

title: Test/seed data leaking to production expert listings
description: Find an Expert grid (/experts) shows production cards with: 'Willy's Welding' located in 'Your Mom, CA' (joke seed); expert bios literally reading 'Test account for plumbing user testing agent.'; all experts have (0)-star ratings; all priced uniformly $75/hr and $15/Q&A.
user_impact: As an intermediate about to spend money ($15+) on a question, this signals 'beta product, no real users'. Trust killer at the moment of conversion.
expected: Real expert listings only on production; or clearly mark beta-seeded accounts with a 'Test / Beta' badge.
fix_recommendation: Add 'is_test_account' flag on expert profiles; filter from production marketplace. Add 'Newly joined' badge for low/zero-review experts instead of bald (0).

title: Expert detail page CTA shows '$15' even when first question is free
description: On /experts/:id, the action button reads 'Ask a Paid Question — $15'. Click into the form and a clear green promo says 'Your first question is free! No payment method needed... FREE — First question on us!'. The CTA price ($15) on the expert page mismatches the actual price ($0) on the form.
user_impact: I expected to pay $15, was pleasantly surprised on next page that it's free. But the CTA mismatch makes me wonder if pricing is sloppy across the platform.
expected: CTA should reflect actual price for me, e.g., 'Ask a Question — First one free!' for eligible users, '$15' for users past their freebie.
fix_recommendation: Check first-question-free eligibility in expert detail page, conditionally render CTA copy.

title: 'What you get that a phone call can't provide' panel has poor contrast
description: On the Q&A submission form, the 4-point value-prop panel ('Pre-contextualized answer', 'Documented record', 'Payment protection', 'Verified tradespeople') uses slate-blue foreground text on a dark surface. Low contrast, hard to scan.
user_impact: As a fast scanner I almost missed this entirely. The selling points are good but invisible.
expected: WCAG AA contrast minimum for body text.
fix_recommendation: Bump foreground to brighter token (earth-cream or copper) or increase weight. Check contrast in design-system audit.

title: 'My Questions' empty state appears below active submission form on Q&A page
description: On /marketplace/qa with a target expert, the page shows the question composition form. Scrolling down reveals 'My Questions' empty state ('No questions yet... Talk to a Pro') directly below the form, competing for attention while user is composing.
